title: Executive Order 11890—Amending Executive Order No. 10422, as Amended, Prescribing Procedures for Making Available to the Secretary General of the United Nations Certain Information Concerning United States Citizens Employed or Being Considered for Employment on the Secretariat of the United Nations

flags.justification: This narrows rather than expands government screening authority and explicitly incorporates Privacy Act of 1974 compliance throughout.
Clear, specific amendments to the investigation procedure.
Multiple related amendments (investigation procedure, disqualification standard) coherently serve one purpose.
The new disqualification standard requires 'specific intent' and 'unlawfully' advocating or practicing force or violence, a materially more precise standard than a bare-association or advocacy test.
No incentive structure rewarding behavior contrary to the order's purpose is identifiable.
This adds Privacy Act compliance requirements rather than removing any existing oversight.
No third-party compliance regime is created.

This narrows a Cold War-era loyalty-screening standard originally issued in 1953 to require specific intent and unlawful conduct rather than mere association or advocacy, a significant, rights-protective narrowing of governmental screening power comparable to EO 11785's dismantling of the Attorney General's list.
No second agency's competing mandate is implicated.
No coverage population or exemption structure is created.

notes: Validation batch (negative). Structural weight: 1/14 = 7.1%. A third instance of the 'power-narrowing reads clean' pattern already logged for EO 11785: a loyalty-security screening standard originating in the McCarthy era is narrowed to require specific intent and unlawful conduct, and the order scores low precisely because it reduces rather than expands governmental power over individuals.
